title: Contact Structures, foliations and group actions
speaker: Jonathan Bowden
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Foliation-contact dictionary for cooriented plane fields
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a closed oriented 3-manifold with a cooriented 2-plane field and compares foliations and contact structures through the Frobenius equality versus the contact inequality, their local normal forms, holonomy signs, and the intermediate notion of confoliation.

proof_stages.label: Flexibility via Reeb components and overtwisted disks
proof_stages.summary: Classical existence and uniqueness results are reviewed: arbitrary plane fields can be homotoped to foliated or contact plane fields by inserting Reeb components or overtwisted disks, while overtwisted contact structures and certain foliations exhibit h-principle-type deformation flexibility.

proof_stages.label: Eliashberg-Thurston approximation bridge
proof_stages.summary: The Eliashberg-Thurston approximation theorem and later refinements are used to explain how suitable foliations admit nearby positive and negative contact structures, with tautness yielding weak fillability and tightness of the approximations.

proof_stages.label: Topology of taut and horizontal foliation spaces
proof_stages.summary: The main problem is formulated as understanding the topology of spaces of taut foliations, especially horizontal foliations on circle bundles over surfaces, by trying to invert the approximation bridge and detect nonexistence of foliation paths using contact invariants.

proof_stages.label: Linear contactization of foliation paths
proof_stages.summary: A linear deformation lemma says that, when a foliation has no compact leaves and the 3-manifold does not fiber over S^1, one can choose a 1-form beta so that alpha+t beta is contact for small nonzero t, and convexity lets this be done along smooth families.

proof_stages.label: Disconnected representation components and endpoint actions
proof_stages.summary: Using holonomy of horizontal foliations, the speaker states a non-path-connectedness theorem for intermediate nonzero Euler-class representation spaces of surface groups into orientation-preserving circle diffeomorphisms and constructs the two key endpoint actions: a collapsed lower-genus PSL(2,R) lattice action and a k-fold lift of a Fuchsian action.

proof_stages.label: Suspension-to-contact contradiction using twisting
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a path between the endpoint representations, suspension gives a smooth path of horizontal foliations satisfying the linear lemma’s hypotheses, hence a contact deformation, but carefully chosen endpoint perturbations have different Giroux twisting invariants, giving a contradiction.

proof_stages.label: C0 variants and epsilon-contact action spaces
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes with extensions and questions, including a homeomorphism/C0 version, a lifted representation formulation of Euler class via commutators, and intermediate epsilon-inequality spaces interpolating between foliated representations and horizontal contact structures.

examples.name: Changing-slope foliations on the 3-torus
examples.note: The family of foliations on T^3 with varying slope is used as a counterexample to the naive claim that every foliation deformation can be approximated by a contact deformation, thereby motivating extra hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: The changing-slope foliations on the 3-torus are invoked only as a counterexample to the sketchy approximation idea, without tracing the slope change or analyzing the foliations.

passes.reason: standard slope-changing torus foliations are highly regular/translation-invariant and are cited to show the naive foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in full generality
Used to show the proposed passage from foliation deformations to contact deformations fails; the slope-changing torus foliation model is a highly regular linear/torus example.
A highly regular slope family on T^3 is cited to show the naive deformation-of-foliations-to-deformation-of-contact-structures idea fails in general.
Used as a path of foliations on T^3 changing slope to show the proposed foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in full generality.
Linear changing-slope foliations on the flat 3-torus are translation-regular examples used to show the foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in general.
Ordinary slope-varying torus foliations are cited to show the proposed foliation-path to contact-deformation idea is false in general.
It is invoked as the standard T^3 slope-changing deformation that defeats the naive contact-deformation argument, and the torus foliations are homogeneous.
Used to show the proposed foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in full generality; the constant-slope torus foliations are highly regular/translation-invariant.
used to show the naive passage from foliation deformations to contact deformations fails; the T^3 slope-changing foliations are highly regular linear/torus-symmetric examples.
used to show that foliation deformations cannot in general be promoted to contact deformations, via a homogeneous slope-changing family on T^3
A highly regular linear-slope family on T^3 is used to show the naive foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in full generality.
Used to show the foliation-to-contact deformation idea fails in full generality; the standard changing-slope foliations on T^3 are linear/translation-regular.

examples.name: Maximal Euler-class Fuchsian component
examples.note: The maximal Euler-class case is recalled as a contrasting known case where Ghys rigidity gives connectedness, showing why the theorem concerns intermediate Euler classes.
examples.engagement_reason: The maximal Euler-class Fuchsian component is cited via Ghys’s connectedness/rigidity result, but the speaker does not derive or compute anything in that component.

passes.reason: the Fuchsian component has maximal Euler class at the Milnor-Wood bound and is used as a known rigid endpoint rather than a disproof
The Fuchsian component has Euler class attaining the Milnor–Wood bound, so it is an extremal component rather than a counterexample.
The Fuchsian component realizes the maximal Milnor--Wood Euler class and is used to show the nonconnectedness statement cannot simply include the maximal case.
The Fuchsian component has maximal Euler class, attaining the Milnor-Wood bound, and is cited as a known connected rigid case.
The Fuchsian component attains the Milnor-Wood maximal Euler class and is cited as connected, blocking extension of the nonconnectedness claim to equality.
The Fuchsian component has maximal Euler class, attaining the Milnor–Wood bound, and is used as a known connected boundary case.
It is the Milnor-Wood equality case of maximal Euler class, used as a connected Fuchsian comparison rather than a refutation.
The maximal Euler-class case attains the Milnor--Wood bound and is cited as connected, so the non-connectedness statement cannot be extended to equality.
the Fuchsian component is the Milnor-Wood maximal Euler-class boundary case, invoked as a rigid connected comparison rather than as a refutation.
serves as the Milnor--Wood equality case with maximal Euler class, a connected extremal comparison rather than a refutation of the stated claim
The maximal Milnor-Wood Euler-class Fuchsian component is the boundary case where Ghys gives connectedness, obstructing extension of the non-connectedness statement to maximal Euler class.
The maximal Euler-class Fuchsian component saturates the Milnor–Wood bound and is cited as the rigid connected endpoint, not as a refutation of the stated nonmaximal result.

examples.name: Zero Euler class and commuting circle diffeomorphisms
examples.note: The zero Euler-class case, including commuting diffeomorphisms for the torus group, is mentioned to mark an unknown boundary case outside the stated nonconnectedness result.
examples.engagement_reason: The zero Euler-class case is identified with the difficult setting of commuting circle diffeomorphisms, but no construction or property is checked on a specific commuting example.

passes.reason: zero Euler class is the topologically trivial/abelian commuting-diffeomorphism case and is mentioned as an open difficult case rather than a counterexample
The zero-Euler-class/torus case is a vanishing, base-case situation leading to commuting circle diffeomorphisms, mentioned as a hard open case rather than as a refutation.
The zero-Euler-class torus/commuting-diffeomorphism case is a vanishing, low-complexity base case mentioned as a hard open regime rather than as a disproof.
The zero Euler-class case is the untwisted/abelian commuting-diffeomorphism boundary case mentioned as hard and unknown, not as a disproof.
Euler class zero is the trivial/minimal-twisting boundary case, including abelian torus-group actions by commuting circle diffeomorphisms, mentioned as an unknown hard case rather than a disproof.
Zero Euler class is the untwisted/abelian base case leading to commuting circle diffeomorphisms, mentioned as an unknown case rather than a refutation.
It is the trivial Euler-class/torus-group case leading to commuting circle diffeomorphisms, mentioned as a hard open regime.
The zero Euler-class case is presented as an excluded open/unknown regime, with vanishing twisting reducing in the torus case to commuting circle diffeomorphisms.
the zero-Euler-class/torus case has vanishing twisting data and reduces to abelian actions by commuting circle diffeomorphisms, mentioned as an open hard case.
used as an untwisted zero-Euler-class low-complexity case leading to commuting circle diffeomorphisms, not as an adversarial example
The zero-Euler-class case is the trivial/abelian regime, reducing for the torus to commuting circle diffeomorphisms, and is mentioned as a hard open case rather than a refutation.
The zero-Euler/torus case collapses to an abelian commuting-diffeomorphism problem and is presented as a hard boundary case rather than a counterexample.
